title: Faraday's law - magnitude of induced emf (average)
description: Let's solve some problems to better understand how to calculate the average induced emf in a coil exposed to a changing flux.
topic_path: Faraday's law - magnitude of induced emf (average)

assessment_items.question_content:
A current loop having area $A=4.0\ \text m^2$ is moving in a non-uniform magnetic field as shown. In $5.0\ \text s$, it moves from an area having magnetic field magnitude $B_i=0.20\ \text T$ to one having greater magnitude $B_f$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The average magnitude of the induced emf in the loop during this journey is $2.0\ \text V$.

**Find $B_f$.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text T$
hints:
##Strategy

The average emf induced in the loop depends on how much the flux $\Phi$ changes over a period of time. Using Faraday's law which describes this relation, we will find the required value $B_f$.

##Solution

Let us first calculate the change in flux using Faraday's law.

$\begin{align}
\blueE {|\varepsilon_\text{avg}|}&=\dfrac{{|\Delta \Phi|}}{\redE {\Delta t}}\\\\
\Rightarrow |\Delta \Phi| &=\blueE {|\varepsilon_{\text{avg}}|}\times\redE{\Delta t}\\\\
&=\blueE{2.0\ \text V} \times \redE{5.0\ \text s}\\\\
&=10\ \text {Wb}
 \end{align}$

Now, using $\Phi=BA \cos \theta$, we can say

$\begin{align} 
 |\Delta \Phi|  &= |\goldE {\Phi_f}-\maroonD{\Phi_i}| \\\\
 &= |\goldE {B_fA\cos\theta}-\maroonD{B_iA\cos\theta}|\\\\
&=|B_f-B_i||A||\cos\theta|\\\\
&=|B_f-0.20\ \text T|\times4.0\ \text m^2\times 1
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now, using the value of $|\Delta \Phi|$ that we obtained previously, we can find $B_f$.

Putting $\Delta \Phi=10\ \text{Wb}$,

$\begin{align} 
&\Rightarrow 10\ \text{Wb} =|B_f-0.20\ \text T|\times4.0\ \text m^2\times 1\\\\
&\Rightarrow |B_f-0.20\ \text T| =\dfrac{\stackrel{2.5}{\cancel {10}}}{\cancel {4.0}}\ \text T
\end{align}$

Since $B_f$ is greater than $0.20\ \text T$,

$\begin{align} 
\Rightarrow B_f-0.20\ \text T &=2.5\ \text T\\\\
\Rightarrow B_f &=2.7\ \text T
\end{align}$

The value of $B_f$ is:

$\boxed{2.7}\ \text T$.

assessment_items.question_content:
As shown, a current loop having area $A=4.0\ \text m^2$ is moving in a non-uniform magnetic field. In $8.0\ \text s$, it moves from an area having magnetic field magnitude $B_i=0.70\ \text T$ to one having greater magnitude $B_f$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The average magnitude of the induced emf in the loop during this journey is $0.50\ \text V$.

**Find $B_f$.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text T$
hints:
##Strategy

The average emf induced in the loop depends on how much the flux $\Phi$ changes over a period of time. Using Faraday's law which describes this relation, we will find the required value $B_f$.

##Solution

Let us first calculate the change in flux using Faraday's law.

$\begin{align}
\blueE {|\varepsilon_\text{avg}|}&=\dfrac{{|\Delta \Phi|}}{\redE {\Delta t}}\\\\
\Rightarrow |\Delta \Phi| &=\blueE {|\varepsilon_{\text{avg}}|}\times\redE{\Delta t}\\\\
&=\blueE{0.50\ \text V} \times \redE{8.0\ \text s}\\\\
&=4.0\ \text {Wb}
 \end{align}$

Now, using $\Phi=BA \cos \theta$, we can say

$\begin{align} 
 |\Delta \Phi|  &= |\goldE {\Phi_f}-\maroonD{\Phi_i}| \\\\
 &= |\goldE {B_fA\cos\theta}-\maroonD{B_iA\cos\theta}|\\\\
&=|B_f-B_i||A||\cos\theta|\\\\
&=|B_f-0.70\ \text T|\times4.0\ \text m^2\times 1
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now, using the value of $|\Delta \Phi|$ that we obtained previously, we can find $B_f$.

Putting $\Delta \Phi=10\ \text{Wb}$,

$\begin{align} 
&\Rightarrow 4.0\ \text{Wb} =|B_f-0.70\ \text T|\times4.0\ \text m^2\times 1\\\\
&\Rightarrow |B_f-0.70\ \text T| =\dfrac{\stackrel{1}{\cancel {4.0}}}{\cancel {4.0}}\ \text T
\end{align}$

Since $B_f$ is greater than $0.70\ \text T$,

$\begin{align} 
\Rightarrow B_f-0.70\ \text T &=1.0\ \text T\\\\
\Rightarrow B_f &=1.7\ \text T
\end{align}$

The value of $B_f$ is:

$\boxed{1.7}\ \text T$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The rectangular loop shown below moves into a region of uniform magnetic field at a speed of $v=10\ \dfrac{\text{cm}}{\text s}$. 

[[☃ image 1]]

The dimensions of the rectangle are $l=5\ \text{cm}$ and $b=10\ \text{cm}$. The magnitude of the field is $B=0.1\ \text{T}$.

**Find the magnitude of the average emf induced in the loop as it fully enters the magnetic field.**

$|\varepsilon_{\text{avg}}|=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\times 10^{-3}\ \text V$
hints:
##Strategy

The average emf induced in the loop depends on how much the flux $\Phi$ changes as the rectangle enters the loop. So, we need to use the time it takes for the rectangle to enter the loop in Faraday's law to calculate the required emf.

##Solution

First, let us find the time $\Delta t$ it takes for the rectangle to enter the loop. The rectangle moves $10\ \text {cm}$ to the right every second. To enter the magnetic field completely, it needs to move $5\ \text{cm}$ to the right. So it takes $\Delta t=\dfrac{1}{2}\ \text s$ for the loop to enter the field.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now let us calculate the change In flux as the loop enters the field.

Initially, the loop is completely outside the field, so $\maroonD {\Phi_i}=0$.

Finally the loop is completely inside the field, so using $\Phi=BA \cos \theta$, we can say



$\begin{align} 
|\goldE {\Phi_f}| &= {BA|\cos\theta|}\\\\
&=B\times{(lb)}\times|\cos \theta|\\\\
&=0.1\ \text T\times(5\ \text{cm}\times10\ \text{cm})\ \times 1\\\\
&=0.1\ \text T \times 50 \times 10^{-4}\ \text m^2\\\\
&=5\times10^{-4}\ \text{Wb}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now, using Faraday's law,

$\begin{align}
\blueE {|\varepsilon_\text{avg}|}&=\dfrac{{|\Delta \Phi|}}{\redE {\Delta t}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{|\goldE{\Phi_f}-\maroonD{\Phi_i}|}{\redE {\Delta t}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\goldE{5\times10^{-4}\ \text{Wb}}}{\redE{\dfrac{1}{2}\ \text s}}\\\\
&=10^{-3}\ \text V
 \end{align}$

The magnitude of the average emf induced in the loop as it fully enters the magnetic field is:

$\boxed{1}\times10^{-3}\ \text V$

assessment_items.question_content:
The rectangular loop shown below moves into a region of uniform magnetic field at a speed of $v=2.5\ \dfrac{\text{cm}}{\text s}$. 

[[☃ image 1]]

The dimensions of the rectangle are $l=10\ \text{cm}$ and $b=40\ \text{cm}$. The magnitude of the field is $B=0.6\ \text{T}$.

**Find the magnitude of the average emf induced in the loop as it fully enters the magnetic field.**

$|\varepsilon_{\text{avg}}|=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\times 10^{-3}\ \text V$
hints:
##Strategy

The average emf induced in the loop depends on how much the flux $\Phi$ changes as the rectangle enters the loop. So, we need to use the time it takes for the rectangle to enter the loop in Faraday's law to calculate the required emf.

##Solution

First, let us find the time $\Delta t$ it takes for the rectangle to enter the loop. The rectangle moves $2.5\ \text {cm}$ to the right every second. To enter the magnetic field completely, it needs to move $10\ \text{cm}$ to the right. So it takes $\Delta t=4.0\ \text s$ for the loop to enter the field.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now let us calculate the change In flux as the loop enters the field.

Initially, the loop is completely outside the field, so $\maroonD {\Phi_i}=0$.

Finally the loop is completely inside the field, so using $\Phi=BA \cos \theta$, we can say



$\begin{align} 
|\goldE {\Phi_f}| &= {BA|\cos\theta|}\\\\
&=B\times{(lb)}\times|\cos \theta|\\\\
&=0.6\ \text T\times(10\ \text{cm}\times40\ \text{cm})\ \times 1\\\\
&=0.6\ \text T \times 400 \times 10^{-4}\ \text m^2\\\\
&=2.4\times10^{-2}\ \text{Wb}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now, using Faraday's law,

$\begin{align}
\blueE {|\varepsilon_\text{avg}|}&=\dfrac{{|\Delta \Phi|}}{\redE {\Delta t}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{|\goldE{\Phi_f}-\maroonD{\Phi_i}|}{\redE {\Delta t}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\goldE{2.4\times10^{-2}\ \text{Wb}}}{\redE{4.0\ \text s}}\\\\
&=6\times10^{-3}\ \text V
 \end{align}$

The magnitude of the average emf induced in the loop as it fully enters the magnetic field is:

$\boxed{6}\times10^{-3}\ \text V$

assessment_items.question_content:
The magnitude of a magnetic field changes with time as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

This field passes perpendicularly through a wire loop having area $A=0.7\ \text m^2$.

**Find the magnitude of the average emf induced in the loop between $t=1\ \text s$ and $t=5\ \text s$.**

$|\varepsilon_{\text{avg}}|=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\times 10^{-2}\ \text V$
hints:
##Strategy

The average emf induced in the loop depends on how much the flux $\Phi$ changes over a period of time. So let us use Faraday's law and find the average emf.

##Solution

First, let us find the initial flux $\maroonD{\Phi_i}$ and final flux $\goldE{\Phi_f}$.

$\begin{align} 
\maroonD{\Phi_i} &=\Phi\ \text{at}\ t=1\ \text s\\\\
&={B(\text{at}\ t=1\ \text s)\times A\cos\theta}\\\\
&=0.8\ \text T\times 0.7\ \text m^2\times \cos \theta
\end{align}$

Similarly, 

$\begin{align} 
\goldE{\Phi_f} &=\Phi\ \text{at}\ t=5\ \text s\\\\
&={B(\text{at}\ t=5\ \text s)\times A\cos\theta}\\\\
&=1.2\ \text T\times 0.7\ \text m^2\times \cos \theta
\end{align}$

Now, using these values let us calculate the magnitude of change in flux.

$\begin{align} 
 |\Delta \Phi|  &= |\goldE {\Phi_f}-\maroonD{\Phi_i}| \\\\
 &= |1.2\ \text T-0.8\ \text T|\times0.7\ \text m^2\times|\cos \theta|\\\\
&=0.4\times0.7\times 1\ \text{Wb}\\\\
&=0.28\ \text{Wb}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now, using Faraday's law,

$\begin{align}
\blueE {|\varepsilon_\text{avg}|}&=\dfrac{{|\Delta \Phi|}}{\redE {\Delta t}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{0.28\ \text{Wb}}{\redE{5\ \text s-1\ \text s}}\\\\
&=7\times10^{-2}\ \text V
 \end{align}$

The magnitude of the average emf induced in the loop is:

$\boxed{7}\times10^{-2}\ \text V$

assessment_items.question_content:
The magnitude of a magnetic field changes with time as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

This field passes perpendicularly through a wire loop having area $A=0.1\ \text m^2$.

**Find the magnitude of the average emf induced in the loop between $t=1\ \text s$ and $t=3\ \text s$.**

$|\varepsilon_{\text{avg}}|=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\times 10^{-2}\ \text V$
hints:
##Strategy

The average emf induced in the loop depends on how much the flux $\Phi$ changes over a period of time. So let us use Faraday's law and find the average emf.

##Solution

First, let us find the initial flux $\maroonD{\Phi_i}$ and final flux $\goldE{\Phi_f}$.

$\begin{align} 
\maroonD{\Phi_i} &=\Phi\ \text{at}\ t=1\ \text s\\\\
&={B(\text{at}\ t=1\ \text s)\times A\cos\theta}\\\\
&=0.5\ \text T\times 0.1\ \text m^2\times \cos \theta
\end{align}$

Similarly, 

$\begin{align} 
\goldE{\Phi_f} &=\Phi\ \text{at}\ t=3\ \text s\\\\
&={B(\text{at}\ t=3\ \text s)\times A\cos\theta}\\\\
&=0.2\ \text T\times 0.1\ \text m^2\times \cos \theta
\end{align}$

Now, using these values let us calculate the magnitude of change in flux.

$\begin{align} 
 |\Delta \Phi|  &= |\goldE {\Phi_f}-\maroonD{\Phi_i}| \\\\
 &= |0.2\ \text T-0.5\ \text T|\times0.1\ \text m^2\times|\cos \theta|\\\\
&=0.3\times0.1\times 1\ \text{Wb}\\\\
&=0.03\ \text{Wb}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now, using Faraday's law,

$\begin{align}
\blueE {|\varepsilon_\text{avg}|}&=\dfrac{{|\Delta \Phi|}}{\redE {\Delta t}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{0.03\ \text{Wb}}{\redE{3\ \text s-1\ \text s}}\\\\
&=1.5\times10^{-2}\ \text V
 \end{align}$

The magnitude of the average emf induced in the loop is:

$\boxed{1.5}\times10^{-2}\ \text V$

assessment_items.question_content:
A current loop rotates in a uniform magnetic field having magnitude $5.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text T$ as shown below. The direction of the area vector $\bold A$ is also shown.

[[☃ image 1]]

The area of the loop is $2.0\times 10^{-3}\ \text m^2$. 

**Find the magnitude of the average emf induced in the loop as it rotates from $\theta =60\degree$ to $\theta =180\degree$ in $0.20\ \text s$.**  
*Note: $\theta$ denotes the angle between the area vector and magnetic field.*

$|\varepsilon_{\text{avg}}|=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\times 10^{-5}\ \text V$
hints:
##Strategy

The average emf induced in the loop depends on how much the flux $\Phi$ changes over a period of time. So let us use Faraday's law and find the average emf.

Now, using Faraday's law,

$\begin{align}
\blueE {|\varepsilon_\text{avg}|}&=\dfrac{{|\Delta \Phi|}}{\redE {\Delta t}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{15\times10^{-6}\ \text{Wb}}{\redE{0.2\ \text s}}\\\\
&=7.5\times10^{-5}\ \text V
 \end{align}$

The magnitude of the average emf induced in the loop is:

$\boxed{7.5}\times10^{-5}\ \text V$

assessment_items.question_content:
A current loop rotates in a uniform magnetic field having magnitude $7.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text T$ as shown below. The direction of the area vector $\bold A$ is also shown.

[[☃ image 1]]

The area of the loop is $4.0\times 10^{-3}\ \text m^2$. 

**Find the magnitude of the average emf induced in the loop as it rotates from $\theta =0\degree$ to $\theta =180\degree$ in $1.0\ \text s$.**  
*Note: $\theta$ denotes the angle between the area vector and magnetic field.*

$|\varepsilon_{\text{avg}}|=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\times 10^{-5}\ \text V$
hints:
##Strategy

The average emf induced in the loop depends on how much the flux $\Phi$ changes over a period of time. So let us use Faraday's law and find the average emf.

Now, using Faraday's law,

$\begin{align}
\blueE {|\varepsilon_\text{avg}|}&=\dfrac{{|\Delta \Phi|}}{\redE {\Delta t}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{60\times10^{-6}\ \text{Wb}}{\redE{1.0\ \text s}}\\\\
&=6.0\times10^{-5}\ \text V
 \end{align}$

The magnitude of the average emf induced in the loop is:

$\boxed{6.0}\times10^{-5}\ \text V$
